title: Chapter 26: The Fitting
content: "I want the world to see you as I see you."

Edward said it like a vow. Like something stolen from a wedding toast, refined and rehearsed, the kind of line that landed in a woman's chest and set up residence there. He was standing in the doorway of Jane's study, one shoulder against the frame, the morning light from the corridor catching the silver at his temples. He looked like a magazine spread. He always looked like a magazine spread. That was part of the problem — the man was so perfectly composed that you forgot to question the composition.
Jane set down her pen. "A fitting?"

"For the Paris show. Closing night." He crossed the room, took her hand, and turned it over as though reading a future in the lines of her palm. "You'll be on my arm, in a Thornfield original. Something made for you. Only you."

She recognized the architecture of it immediately: the intimacy, the specificity, the word *made*. He wasn't offering her a gift. He was proposing a transformation. She had seen the blueprint before — in the archives, in the photographs, in the hollow space behind Bertha's eyes when she spoke about the early days. *He dressed me like a doll. I stopped recognizing my own reflection.* And Blanche, before that — Blanche in the champagne-colored column dress at the Milan after-party, laughing too loud in a gown that suppressed every instinct she had. Jane had thought it was confidence. Now she knew it was capture.
This was what Edward did. He found women with edges and sanded them smooth. He built cages so beautiful you forgot to rattle the bars.
"A custom gown," Jane said carefully. "That's — yes. I'd like that."
His smile was warm. Genuine, as far as anything about Edward was ever genuine. He kissed her knuckles, and the diamond on her finger caught the light like a small, sharp warning.

The atelier was on the second floor, in the east wing — not the basement room where the real work happened, the one with the bolts of fabric and the humming industrial machines and the smell of sizing and sweat. This one was for show. Floor-to-ceiling windows poured light across pale oak floors. A three-panel mirror stood at the far end like an altar. Two dress forms wore half-finished muslins, arms raised in permanent surrender.

Two seamstresses were already waiting when Jane arrived. They introduced themselves only by the precision of their hands. One was older — silver-haired, mouth like a closed purse — and the other was young with dark brows and the quick, absorbed focus of someone who saw bodies as geometry.
"Arms up, please."
Jane raised her arms, and the measuring began.

It was clinical. It was intimate. It was both at once, which was the trick of this entire house — everything couched in professionalism, everything deniable. The tape measure whispered across her ribs, her hips, the inside of her wrist. The older seamstress knelt to measure the distance from her waist to her knee, and the younger one circled behind her, running the tape from the nape of her neck to the small of her back. Pins were placed. Small, sharp, their points pressed through muslin and just barely — just enough — against the skin beneath. Jane felt each one like a punctuation mark. A period at the end of a sentence she hadn't agreed to write.
Then Edward arrived.

He didn't knock. He never knocked. He moved through the room like it was an extension of his own body, and within seconds he was standing beside the dress form, studying the muslin toile the seamstresses had draped over Jane's frame.
"The waist," he said, tapping the fabric at her midsection. "Narrower."
The older seamstress nodded and reached for a pin.

"The shoulders — more open. She carries tension here." He touched the place where Jane's neck met her trapezius, and she felt the warmth of his fingers through the thin muslin. "The fabric should correct that."
*Correct.*
Not complement. Not celebrate. Not honor or enhance or reveal.
*Correct.*

As if her body were a mistake in his patterns. As if the tension she carried — the vigilance, the calculation, the constant low-frequency hum of *pay attention, he's watching, don't slip* — were a flaw in the weave rather than a reasonable response to being measured like a garment waiting to be cut.
"Of course, Monsieur Rochester," the younger seamstress said, and the pins shifted, and the muslin tightened, and Jane stood very still inside the architecture of someone else's vision.
Edward circled her once, twice. He tilted his head. "The neckline — scooped, not boat. I want to see the clavicle." A pause. "She has elegant bones."

The seamstresses made notes. Jane watched her own reflection fragment and reassemble across the three mirror panels: the woman in the center, the woman from the side, the woman from the other side. Three Janes, none of them quite right. Muslin pinned to her frame like a shroud worn inside out.
"Perfect," Edward said. "Carry on."
And he left the way he'd come — without knocking, without ceremony, certain that the world would hold its shape in his absence.
The gown arrived four days later.

It was oatmeal silk — the color of nothing. The color of absence dressed up as elegance. The cut was minimalist: a column from shoulder to ankle, cap sleeves that sat precisely on the point of the arm, a scooped neckline that exposed the architecture of her clavicle just as he'd specified. There was no visible construction. No seams. No darts. No zippers. No buttons. It appeared to have been poured from liquid, solidified around her body by some alchemical process that defied the laws of tailoring.
It was, objectively, a masterpiece.
Jane stepped into it, and the silk slid up her legs, over her hips, across her shoulders, and settled against her skin like a second layer — cool and bloodless and smooth. She turned to the mirror.
The woman looking back was a stranger.

She was elegant. Impeccably turned out. The gown suppressed every line of the body beneath it: the muscular curve of her calves from years of walking the Loire roads, the breadth of her shoulders, the slight asymmetry in her posture where she always leaned a fraction to the left. Gone. All of it. She stood before the mirror in a garment that could have been worn by anyone. Any body. Any woman. That was the point. The gown wasn't designed to express Jane Eyre. It was designed to erase her.

She placed her hands on the silk at her waist and felt nothing. Not the fabric — she felt the fabric, cool and expensive and whispering against her palms — but *herself*. The gown had removed her from the equation. She was a hanger. A vehicle. A body to fill a silhouette Edward had drawn without reference to the person inside it.

This was what he'd done to Bertha. This was what he'd done to Blanche. The pattern was identical. Find a woman with texture and dimension. Smooth her into a surface. Place her in the world as proof that Edward Rochester could refine anything — could take the raw and unpredictable and turn it into something controlled, something *correct*.

Jane stared at her reflection and felt the engagement ring on her left hand, heavy as an anchor. The diamond pressed into her fourth finger like a point of heat, a reminder that the cage she was standing in came with a contract.
Footsteps in the corridor. The door opened without a knock.
Edward.

He stopped behind her. She watched him approach in the mirror — his reflection overlapping hers, his dark head above her pale one, his hands settling on her bare shoulders where the silk met skin. His cologne wrapped around her: sandalwood and black pepper, the same scent that had been in their bedroom, in his car, in the air of every room he'd ever entered and made his own. It was a good scent. An expensive one. That was part of the architecture, too.

He adjusted the neckline — a fraction of an inch, barely perceptible — and the silk shifted against her skin. His fingers lingered at the point of her shoulder, then traced down to her elbow. His touch was proprietary. The way you'd adjust a lapel. The way you'd straighten a painting that had tilted on the wall.
"Perfect," he murmured. His lips were close to her ear. "You see? This is who you were always meant to be."
Jane looked at her reflection — controlled, curated, erased — and opened her mouth. The words she wanted to say were sharp enough to cut silk. She swallowed them.
"It's beautiful," she managed.

It was. That was the horror of it. The cage was gorgeous. The cage was always gorgeous — that was how you stayed inside it. If the bars were ugly, you'd leave. If the lock was obvious, you'd pick it. But when the cage was silk and hand-finished seams and a man who touched your shoulder and called you *perfect*, you convinced yourself it wasn't a cage at all. It was a gown. It was a gift. It was love.
Edward smiled at her reflection. He kissed the top of her head and left.
The door clicked shut.

Jane stood motionless for a long moment. The atelier was quiet save for the distant sound of a machine running somewhere below — the basement, where the real work happened — and the faint tick of the radiator.
Then she moved.

She turned her back to the mirror. She reached for the hem of the gown and pulled it up, over her hips, examining the interior construction. She ran her fingers along the seam allowances, pressed her nails into the points where fabric met fabric, felt for structure beneath the surface.
The lining.

She felt it before she saw it — a separate layer, slightly cooler than the shell, moving independently when she shifted. She traced the boundary between shell and lining, following the seam from hip to waist, and there — *there* — her fingernail caught on something small and metallic.
A zipper.

Hidden at the left waist, concealed beneath a flap of silk so precisely engineered that it was invisible unless you knew to look. A single zipper, running horizontally, that detached the oatmeal shell from the inner lining entirely. The gown was two pieces: an outer shell and an interior structure, joined by this one small mechanism.
Jane tugged the zipper an inch. The oatmeal silk loosened from the lining like a skin separating from muscle.
Her heart hammered.
She could replace the shell. The lining was the structural garment — the bones, the architecture, the thing that held the shape. The shell was just a skin. And skins could be shed.

She imagined it: the oatmeal silk falling away, and beneath it — crimson. Silk the color of arterial blood. The color of warning. The color of the woman Edward had tried to bury, the one with edges and texture and a voice that didn't fit inside a minimalist column gown.
The fitting that was meant to cage her had given her the exact blueprint for escape.

Jane pulled the zipper closed, smoothed the shell back into place, and turned to the mirror. The stranger in oatmeal looked back at her. But Jane smiled — a real smile, sharp and private, the kind that didn't belong in this room or this gown or this house.
She had two weeks.
That night, Jane descended to the basement.

The corridor was dark, and the door at the end was locked, and the sequence was familiar now — two short knocks, one long, the sound of Grace Poole's heavy tread, the click of bolts releasing. The basement room opened onto a different world: warm, cluttered, smelling of turpentine and tea. Bertha was at the worktable, hand-stitching a seam with the ferocious precision of someone who had nothing but time and every reason to be exact.

Jane sat across from her. She described the fitting — the atelier above ground, the seamstresses, the measurements, the oatmeal silk like a shroud worn wrong-side out. She described Edward's hands on her shoulders, the word *correct*, the woman in the mirror she didn't recognize.
Bertha listened without interrupting. Her needle never stopped.
Then Jane told her about the zipper.

"The lining is separate," Jane said. "The whole shell detaches. There's a hidden zipper at the waist — it's designed for quick costume changes, probably. Models do it all the time during shows. But the construction means I can replace the outer layer without altering the fit. I can walk into that show in his gown, and walk out in something else."
Bertha set down her needle. She leaned back in her chair and looked at Jane — really looked, the way she looked at a garment before she deconstructed it, finding the logic in the seams.
Then she laughed.

It was a real laugh. Not the careful, measured sound she usually made — this was dark and full and delighted, the kind of laugh that came from somewhere deep in the chest, from the part of a person that had survived things and found them funny anyway. The sound filled the basement room and bounced off the fabric bolts and the half-finished garments, and Grace Poole glanced up from her book in the corner with raised eyebrows.
"He built you a costume with a secret exit," Bertha said. Her eyes were bright. Fierce. "That man has been designing his own downfall for years and he doesn't even know it."
"He still thinks I'm the woman in the oatmeal gown," Jane said.

"He thinks you're the woman in *any* gown he puts you in." Bertha picked up her needle again, but she was smiling — a knife's smile, thin and sure. "He's never once considered that the garment might have its own opinion."

Jane leaned forward. "I can do this. At the show. I walk in wearing his design — the Thornfield gown, the *correct* silhouette, everything he wants the world to see. And then, at the critical moment—" She made a motion with her hands: a zipper pulling, a shell falling, a new silhouette emerging. "Crimson. Something that's *mine*."
Bertha's smile widened. "Two women," she said. "Two transformations. Two garments that were meant to cage us, turned into declarations."
"Repurposed," Jane said.

"Purposed correctly." Bertha's needle resumed its rhythm — in, out, in, out — but her eyes stayed on Jane. "You'll need the crimson shell constructed to match the lining exactly. Same boning channels, same seam placements. I can do that. I know his construction methods better than anyone in that atelier — I taught half of them."
"Can you have it ready in two weeks?"

"I can have it ready in ten days." Bertha's voice was steady. Clinical. This was the part of her that had survived the attic — not the woman who flinched at footsteps, but the one who had spent years deconstructing Edward's work in secret, learning its logic, memorizing its weaknesses. "The question isn't whether I can build it. The question is whether you can wear it in front of him and not flinch."
Jane thought of the oatmeal silk, cool against her shoulders. The word *correct*, spoken like a compliment. The ring on her finger, heavy as a brand. The stranger in the mirror.
"I can flinch later," she said. "Right now, I have a showstopper to deliver."

Bertha nodded. She reached beneath the worktable and pulled out a bolt of fabric — silk, the color of venous blood, rich and deep and impossible to ignore. She must have been holding it for days. Waiting.

"It's not enough to walk out of his gown," Bertha said, unspooling a length of the crimson silk across the table. "You have to make sure every person in that room sees what's underneath. Not just the dress. The *choice*. The moment the zipper opens and the oatmeal falls — that's the moment Edward loses control of the narrative. And once he loses it, he doesn't get it back."
Jane touched the crimson silk. It was warmer than the oatmeal — somehow, impossibly, as though the color itself generated heat. It slid across her palm like a promise.
"Two weeks," she said.
"Two weeks," Bertha repeated. "And then we show Paris exactly who we are."

The basement hummed around them — the machine noise from somewhere deeper, the scratch of Bertha's needle, the small and secret sound of a plan taking shape inside a cage that didn't know it had a door.
Two weeks. The zipper was hidden. Jane had the blueprint. And the curtain was rising on the final act.
